summary: Stellar Sugaree, epic Iko→Dew, tight second‑set jams
blurb: The show delivers a scorching first set highlighted by New Minglewood Blues and a soaring Sugaree, then launches into an unforgettable second‑set Iko Iko → Morning Dew transition that rolls into a powerful Space → Sugar Magnolia jam. Jerry’s guitar is on fire, Brent’s keyboards add color, and the rhythm section drives the energy throughout.
review: On September 2, 1980 at Rochester’s Community War Memorial Auditorium the Dead turned in one of their most electrifying late‑summer performances. The first set rides a wave of momentum from a ripping New Minglewood Blues into a Sugaree that many reviewers call "smoking" and a seamless China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider transition, all buoyed by Brent’s lush Rhodes flourishes. The second set is the real highlight: after a slow‑burning Althea the band erupts into an Iko Iko → Morning Dew sequence that many fans describe as "epic" and "emotional," followed by a thunderous drum break that launches a soaring Space → Sugar Magnolia jam, often cited as one of the best post‑drums passages since ’74. Jerry’s tone is full‑bodied and his solos on Sugaree, Iko Iko and Dew are consistently praised, while Phil’s bass, though sometimes buried in SBD mixes, shines in audience recordings. Brent’s keyboard work adds depth throughout, and both Bill and Mickey lay down a driving, thunderous rhythm that fuels the night’s improvisations. Across all available sources the performance is hailed as a must‑listen showcase of late‑'80 Dead energy.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Strong positive consensus: 7 of 9 recordings label the show as positive, repeatedly praising Sugaree, the Iko Iko → Morning Dew transition and the Space → Sugar Magnolia jam; only two recordings note mixed quality but still highlight key moments. This level of acclaim warrants a high sentiment boost.

best_recording.reason: Good‑quality soundboard capture of the full show; despite slight thinness it remains the most complete and widely endorsed source for this performance.

key_highlights: Smoking Sugaree that many call a standout moment
Epic Iko Iko → Morning Dew transition in the second set
Powerful Space → Sugar Magnolia jam after the drum break
Brent’s sweeping keyboard flourishes and New Minglewood Blues slide work

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia delivers soaring tones and fiery solos on Sugaree, Iko Iko and Morning Dew; his guitar is consistently praised as full‑bodied and emotive throughout the night.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh provides a solid low end that shines in audience recordings ('thunder juice'), though his bass can be buried in some soundboard mixes.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s slide on New Minglewood Blues and rhythmic support are noted; vocal performance is described as weaker than usual on a few tracks.
band_performance.Brent: Mydland’s Rhodes and piano add lush color, especially early in the first set, with his keyboard flourishes highlighted as key to the era’s sound.
band_performance.Mickey: Hart delivers thunderous, driving beats that propel the jams, particularly during the post‑drums Space → Sugar Magnolia segment.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann supplies a powerful drum break that fuels the Iko Iko jam and maintains relentless energy throughout.
